17: A shadow: 'But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.' The death-warning is the first statement of the law-death nexus that Paul develops in Romans 5-6 and 1 Corinthians 15: sin brings death, death reigns through Adam. The prohibition is not arbitrary but reflects the order of creation: the creature's flourishing depends on remaining within the Creator's boundaries. The shadow: as Adam's transgression brought death, Christ's obedience brings life (Romans 5:18-19). The new tree — the cross — reverses the curse of the old tree.
1: The completion of creation — “the heavens and the earth were completed in all their vast array” — finds its NT echo in Christ’s cry from the cross: “It is finished” (tetelestai, John 19:30). The same divine completion that marks the end of creation week is claimed by Christ as the completion of redemption. The new creation is inaugurated in Christ’s finished work just as the original creation was completed in God’s finished work here.
2: God rested on the seventh day. The author of Hebrews develops this as a type of the eschatological rest Christ provides: “There remains, then, a Sabbath-rest for the people of God; for anyone who enters God’s rest also rests from their works, just as God did from his” (Hebrews 4:9–10). The divine rest is not inactivity but completion — the rest of a work fully accomplished. Christ’s resurrection and session at the Father’s right hand (Hebrews 1:3 — “he sat down”) is the posture of completed work. The seventh-day rest is a type of the rest that Christ has achieved and into which believers enter by faith.
3: God blessed and made holy the seventh day. The Sabbath as a day set apart foreshadows the eschatological holy rest that Christ provides. Colossians 2:16–17 states that the Sabbath is “a shadow of the things that were to come; the reality, however, is found in Christ.” The holiness of the seventh day — its being set apart from ordinary time — anticipates the new age that Christ inaugurates, the age that does not end at sundown and is not followed by another ordinary week. Christ himself is the Lord of the Sabbath (Mark 2:28), its fulfillment and its goal.
4: The second toledot formula (“This is the account of the heavens and the earth”) opens the Eden narrative. Transitional as it is, the toledot structure runs through Genesis as the literary frame for the story of God working through chosen lines to carry his purpose forward. The final toledot is the lineage that leads to Christ (Matthew 1:1 opens with a “book of the genesis/genealogy of Jesus Christ” — directly echoing the Genesis toledot formula). This verse is the gate that opens the account of how sin entered the good creation that Christ will redeem.
5: No shrub had appeared, no plant had sprung up — the ground required a worker. The “man to work the ground” (ʾāḏām laʿăbōḏ ʾeṯ-hāʾăḏāmāh) is both Adam and his descendants; ultimately it is the second Adam who truly tends the field of the world. Jesus speaks of himself as one whose Father “is always at his work” (John 5:17) and uses agricultural labor as a persistent image of his mission (John 4:35–38). The unfilled creation that awaits a worker is a pointer to the one who will ultimately make it fruitful.
6: Streams watered the ground — provision through the earth rather than from the sky. The water that the Creator provides anticipates Christ as the source of living water: “whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst. Indeed, the water I give them will become in them a spring of water welling up to eternal life” (John 4:14). The streams of Eden point toward the river of the water of life flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb (Revelation 22:1).
7: A type of the greatest significance: “God formed the man from the dust of the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life.” Paul explicitly contrasts this verse with Christ: “The first man Adam became a living being; the last Adam, a life-giving spirit” (1 Corinthians 15:45). Adam received the breath of life and became a living soul; Christ gives the Spirit of life (John 20:22 — Jesus breathed on his disciples and said “receive the Holy Spirit”). Adam was formed from dust; Christ took on dust (flesh) in the incarnation. The one who received life becomes the one who gives it.
8: The Garden of Eden as the place of divine-human communion points toward the restoration of that communion in Christ. John 20 places the resurrection appearances in a garden (Genesis 20:15 — Mary thinks Jesus is the gardener). Christ is the true gardener who tends and keeps the new creation. The garden as the meeting place of God and humanity is restored in Christ: the final vision of Scripture is a city with a garden at its center, where God dwells with his people (Rev 21–22), with Christ as the Lamb in its midst (Revelation 21:22).
9: The tree of life stands at the center of the garden. The tree of life is Christ’s own promise to the faithful: “To the one who is victorious, I will give the right to eat from the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God” (Revelation 2:7). The tree that was lost through Adam’s fall is restored through Christ’s victory. The cross itself is sometimes called a tree (1 Peter 2:24 — “he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree”), making Christ’s death the means by which the tree of life is reopened. The tree of life that Adam could not keep becomes what Christ gives freely to those who overcome through him.
10: The river flowing from Eden divides into four headwaters, watering the whole known world. The image of a river proceeding from God’s dwelling place reaches its culmination in Revelation 22:1–2: “the river of the water of life, as clear as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb” with the tree of life on either side. Christ is the source of the living water that flows from the place of God’s presence (John 7:38–39 — from within the believer flow rivers of living water, a reference to the Spirit).
11: The land of Havilah with its gold is a geographical detail of Eden’s abundance. The gold of that land being described as “good” points forward to the gold of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:18, 21), which is pure and clear as glass. The material abundance of Eden — gold, resin, onyx — anticipates the surpassing material glory of the restored creation that Christ will bring. The good things of the original garden are not destroyed but glorified in the new creation.
12: The gold, aromatic resin, and onyx of Havilah are part of the original creation’s abundance. Precious materials in Scripture mark sacred space: gold, onyx, and fine stones recur in the tabernacle and temple construction. The materials of Eden anticipate the materials of God’s dwelling with his people, climaxing in the bejeweled city of Rev 21. The original paradise’s wealth points toward the new paradise that Christ brings — a place where the wealth of the nations flows to the dwelling of God (Revelation 21:24, 26).
13: The Gihon river winding through Cush — a geographical anchor for the Eden narrative within the real world. The Gihon is also the name of the spring beside which Solomon was anointed king (1 Kings 1:33–34), a possible echo of the Edenic river. The geographical grounding of the paradise narrative establishes that the fall and redemption are not myths but history — the same world that Christ enters in the incarnation.
14: The Tigris and Euphrates are the great rivers of Mesopotamia — the historical heartland of ANE civilization and the region from which Abraham was called. The Eden narrative is anchored in geography that connects to the Abrahamic calling and ultimately to the whole biblical story. The rivers of paradise are real-world rivers; the promised return to paradise through Christ involves the renewal of the real earth (Revelation 21:1 — new heaven and new earth), not escape from it.
15: The man placed in the garden “to work it and take care of it” uses vocabulary (ʿāḇaḏ and šāmar) that later appears for Levitical priestly service in the tabernacle (Numbers 3:7–8; Genesis 18:7 — “you shall perform the duties of the sanctuary… tend the tabernacle”). Adam is the first priest-king, placed in the garden-sanctuary to guard and tend God’s presence. Christ is the ultimate priest-king (Heb 7) who fulfills what Adam failed — he guards and tends the new temple that is the people of God, and leads them into the true rest (Hebrews 4:14).
16: God’s generous command — “You are free to eat from any tree in the garden” — establishes abundant permission before the single prohibition. The generosity of the Creator contrasts with the serpent’s misrepresentation in Genesis 3:1 (“Did God really say you must not eat from any tree?”). Christ restores the original generosity: “I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full” (John 10:10). The abundance of the gospel exceeds even the garden’s abundance — Christ gives not only physical food but himself as the bread of life (John 6:51).
18: “It is not good for the man to be alone.” The first divine “not good” in Scripture identifies the incompleteness of solitary humanity and drives the creation of Eve. Paul in Ephesians 5:25–32 reads the entire Adam-Eve relationship as a type of Christ and the church: Christ loves the church as a husband loves his wife; he gives himself for her. The solution to man’s aloneness is the companion formed from his own body — which Paul explicitly applies to Christ: “This is a profound mystery — but I am talking about Christ and the church” (Ephesians 5:32). The “not good” that drives creation of the bride drives the entire redemptive plan.
19: Adam’s naming of the animals expresses his God-given authority over creation. Christ, as the second Adam, exercises this authority in full: he names his own sheep (John 10:3 — “he calls his own sheep by name”); he gives new names to those who overcome (Revelation 2:17 — “I will give them a white stone with a new name”). The Adam who named the animals is the type of the Christ who knows and names every individual who belongs to him.
20: “But for Adam no suitable helper was found.” The incompleteness drives forward to the creation of Eve and, through Paul’s typology, to the church as Christ’s companion. Christ does not remain alone: he promised not to leave his disciples as orphans (John 14:18) and sent the Holy Spirit as the Paraclete (Helper/Companion, John 14:16–17). The “suitable helper” for Adam foreshadows the Spirit as helper for Christ’s people and the church as Christ’s bride, through whom his purposes are fulfilled in the world.
21: A type: the deep sleep (Heb. tardēmāh) that fell on Adam while God took his rib and formed Eve foreshadows Christ’s death on the cross. Just as Eve was taken from Adam’s side while he slept, so the church is formed from Christ’s pierced side: “one of the soldiers pierced Jesus’ side with a spear, bringing a sudden flow of blood and water” (John 19:34). The blood and water from Christ’s side are the sacramental signs of the church (baptism and eucharist). The bride formed from the sleeping groom’s side is one of the most ancient and robust Christological readings of this passage, warranted by the Ephesian typology.
22: God made the woman from the rib and brought her to the man. The presentation of the bride to the husband is the type of which Ephesians 5:27 is the antitype: Christ presents “a radiant church, without stain or wrinkle or any other blemish, but holy and blameless” to himself. The church is the bride formed from Christ, presented in holiness through his own sacrifice. Revelation 21:9–10 describes John being shown “the bride, the wife of the Lamb” — the church in her final glory, the ultimate fulfillment of what began when God brought the first bride to her husband.
23: “Bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh.” Paul quotes the Eve-narrative in Ephesians 5:30 and applies it to Christ and the church: “we are members of his body.” The union of Adam and Eve — flesh of each other’s flesh — is the type of the organic, indissoluble union between Christ and the church. As Eve is not a separate creation but is taken from Adam and united to him, so the church is not separate from Christ but united to him in one body. The intimacy of “bone of my bones” is the intimacy that Christ has with his people — a union not of mere affection but of shared nature (2 Peter 1:4 — partakers of the divine nature).
24: Paul calls this verse “a profound mystery—but I am talking about Christ and the church” (Ephesians 5:31–32). The leaving of father and mother, the cleaving to the wife, the becoming one flesh — all three are types of Christ’s union with the church. Christ “left” the Father in the incarnation; he is “united” to the church through the Spirit who joins believers to him (1 Corinthians 6:17); they become “one spirit” with him. This is the most explicitly Christological verse in Genesis 2 — Paul does not impose the typology but declares it the verse’s deepest referent.
25: Adam and Eve’s nakedness without shame is the sign of pre-fall integrity — no guilt, no need for concealment, no broken relationship between the creature and Creator. The fall will introduce shame (Genesis 3:7); Christ’s redemption removes it. The glory that God clothes believers with is described in terms of a robe: “I counsel you to buy from me… white clothes to wear, so you can cover your shameful nakedness” (Revelation 3:18). The shameless nakedness of the garden is the state of full communion with God that Christ restores — not by returning to innocence but by clothing the redeemed in his own righteousness (Isaiah 61:10; Revelation 7:14).
